text: Welcome to This is My Architecture New Zealand.
I'm Viril from AWS and joining me today,
I have Chris from Vodafone New Zealand.
Welcome, Chris.
Thanks, Viril, for having me.
Chris, why don't we start off by you telling us a little bit
about Vodafone New Zealand and your journey to here.
Thanks. So early 2020,
we started on our migration journey from existing on-prem legacy
contact center solution into AWS.
Our existing solution suffered from a number of drawbacks.
Number one, the complexity of the solution ended up meaning
that we had to keep adding to it.
It was very costly and expensive to change,
and that then drove complexity back into the application.
And in the end, by the time we decided to migrate,
the solution itself consisted of over 81,000 different ways
that a customer can interact with our services and products.
That's a huge number.
Additionally to that, we had no visibility of what customers
were doing inside our IVR.
So we couldn't change it.
We couldn't be informed about how that change would actually
affect our customers.
Migrating to the cloud, we had to rethink how that could be changed
and how we could support that in our AWS landscape.
Chris, could you tell us a little bit about the scale?
How many agents, how many calls?
So that solution, the existing solution supported over 2,500 agents,
over three different geographic locations,
and handled over 1 million phone calls per month.
That is a really large scale and a great opportunity for transformation.
81,000 pathways for a customer.
That's some serious complexity.
How did Connect help?
So Connect obviously was our core to our solution.
And us lifting and shifting our existing on-prem solution into Connect
wasn't going to give us anything.
It was just going to move the complexity into the cloud.
So by using Connect and Lex, we're able to simplify the way that customers
actually interacted with our IVR and our solution, both for chat and voice.
Lex, when we started building our intents and matching in it,
only consisted of 200 intents.
So you can imagine, going from 81,000 different ways of navigating
through our product to only 200 ways of both asking for and requesting service
to either products, billing, sales, et cetera.
That's a huge change.
So immediately, we've already simplified landscape a little bit.
Secondly, Connect, we didn't really want to go off and start building contact flows
from day one, just concrete flows.
Every use case was a flow.
We decided to, instead of doing that, move to more of a universal contact flow,
which is modular and able to be stitched together through configuration supplied
by both Lex, sorry, Lambda, and DynamoDB.
That gave us the ability then to externalize a lot of the stitching and mapping
out to an external source and drive those contact flows as we need to.
Awesome.
So you've spoken about the transformation, simplicity, and you've also spoken to a little
bit around automation.
I see Terraform there.
I see developers.
I see DevTools and Connect.
Can you talk us through how that all comes together?
Sure.
So the existing old solution that we were going from, very hard to change, impossible to test.
And when we changed it, we didn't know we were going to break anything.
We had to radically change that to get comfort in the business, to iterate fast, and get that
continuous information feedback so we could change things and know that they're not going
to break and our customers wouldn't be affected.
So to start with that, our developers interacted with our DevTools and used Terraform using
infrastructure as code to automatically promote both ConnectFlows, LexIntense, Lambda, and DynamoDB,
as well as other AWS services into our environment in a no-touch and automated tested pipeline.
It's awesome to see how agility has been wrapped into the solution.
So we've got simplification.
We've got agility.
Talk to us about the data.
Sure.
I mean, Connect itself pumps out a lot of data.
And with Contact Lens, we're getting center analysis.
We're getting automatic transcription, as well as we're getting the CTR attributes.
We're getting contact flow logs, and all this data together is being pumped out to our Snowflake
data warehouse, where we also have a single view of customer.
With this information, we're able to then service it up through dashboards, where agents, team
leads, and developers are able to query the information and understand how customers are
actually interacting with our products.
And critically, the developers are able to be informed about how changes are made, how they
are rolled through environments, and how that continuous information feedback loop is able
to give us improvements.
We're able to measure the improvements, and test whether they're actually having impact
for our customers.
Awesome to see how that loop is having an influence.
What does the future hold for Vodafone New Zealand?
Sure.
So I touched on at the start, the main channels we're dealing with was synchronous chat and
synchronous phone.
Adding to that in the future, we will be adding asynchronous chat through social accounts, through
WhatsApp, through Apple Business Chat, through email and SMS.
This will enhance our reach and get our product into where the customers are most comfortable
dealing with our solution.
Chris, thank you for sharing your architecture with us.
It's really nice to see how Vodafone New Zealand is taking a customer first, agent first, team
lead first, developer first approach to enhancing the user experience for the product that they've
created.
Again, thank you for sharing your architecture with us.
Thanks, very much.
It's been a pleasure.
And thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
